title: Deep Dive into Flashbacks: How They Unfold and Planning Your Own
simple_explanation: We're figuring out how flashbacks work in stories, like how a magic eye can show you past pictures! We also talk about different kinds of stories (genres) and how to plan our own flashback, thinking about 'what's happening now', 'what makes me remember', and 'what happened before'.
detailed_explanation: In the 'Eye of the Wolf' story, the flashback is triggered by the boy looking into the wolf's eye, and the description makes it seem like the eye itself is transforming. This is a very creative way to move into the past. We'll think about different story 'genres' – like fantasy, adventure, or mystery – and discuss which ones 'The Eye of the Wolf' might fit into. Stories can sometimes be a mix of a few! When planning your own stories with flashbacks, it's important to think about what 'normal' is before the flashback, what 'changes' to trigger it, and then what details you want to show from the past. A good plan helps you make sure your flashback is clear and exciting, guiding the reader through the transition from the present to the past smoothly and effectively.

examples_expanded.text: A normal scene might be 'The street was busy.' A change could be 'Then, a strange melody drifted from an open window.' And the flashback might begin 'He remembered his grandmother's lullabies...'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of fiction: stories with flashbacks in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: A fantasy story might have dragons and magic, while a mystery might have detectives solving a puzzle. 'The Eye of the Wolf' has talking animals, so it has elements of fantasy.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of fiction: stories with flashbacks in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: When you describe a flashback, use lots of sensory words – what is seen, heard, felt, smelled, or even tasted in that past moment.
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of fiction: stories with flashbacks in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'harvest moon' in a sentence. Remember it means: A large, bright moon that appears close to the horizon around the time of the autumn harvest.
Match the word 'pupil' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Fiction: Stories with flashbacks.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'harvest moon' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: Flashbacks can be triggered by unusual events or sensory details in the main story.
Writers use vivid descriptions to show how a flashback progresses and what is seen.
Stories can belong to one or more genres (e.g., fantasy, mystery, adventure).
Planning a flashback sequence involves thinking about what's normal, the trigger, and the past details.
Language choices significantly impact how a reader experiences the flashback.

vocabulary.definition: A large, bright moon that appears close to the horizon around the time of the autumn harvest.
The black centre of the eye that lets light in.
The coloured part of the eye around the pupil.
To show or reveal something that was hidden.
A category or type of art, music, or literature (e.g., fantasy, science fiction).
The main events of a story, told as an interrelated sequence.
The place and time at which a story takes place.
